content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Nowadays, AI summaries cut click-through in half. Ranking still helps, but AI recommendation now decides who gets chosen.
Here’s the uncomfortable number: Pew Research Center found that when Google users saw an AI summary, only 8% of them still clicked a traditional search result. Without an AI summary, that click rate was 15%.
That’s why the old digital marketing question, “Are we ranking?”, is becoming dangerously incomplete.
A pattern I keep seeing in visibility audits is simple. A brand ranks for a few service keywords, runs ads, posts content regularly, and still disappears the moment an AI tool gets asked a real buyer-style question.
I’m not talking about a brand-name search. I mean something like: Which performance marketing consultant can help a D2C brand improve ROAS without wasting ad spend?
Usually the problem isn’t one missing keyword. It’s weak proof, vague positioning, unclear category identity, and a digital footprint that doesn’t give AI enough confidence to recommend the brand at all.
For years, every brand wanted one thing from digital marketing: rank on Google. Page one meant visibility. Top three meant authority. Position one meant dominance. SEO teams chased keywords, agencies sent ranking reports, and founders checked search positions like stock prices.
Ranking still matters. But in the AI-search era, it’s no longer the full battle.
The sharper question is this: when someone asks ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Mode, or any AI search tool for a recommendation in your category, does your brand show up?
Earlier, a customer searched best digital marketing agency near me. Now the same customer might type: I run a D2C skincare brand and need help with Meta ads, Google Ads, SEO, ecommerce sales, and lead generation without wasting budget. Who should I consider?
That’s not a keyword. That’s a buying conversation. And in a buying conversation, your brand doesn’t win because one page has the right keyword density. It wins when AI understands what you do, who you serve, why you’re credible, and whether you deserve a spot on the shortlist.
Search is becoming a Recommendation Layer
Traditional search was linear: search, click, compare, decide. AI search breaks that. Now it can summarize, compare, and recommend before anyone reaches your website.
OpenAI built ChatGPT search around fast answers and natural follow-up questions instead of fresh keyword searches each time. Google is moving the same way: at its May 2026 I/O keynote, it said AI Mode had crossed one billion monthly users, and AI Overviews had passed 2.5 billion.
A Google ranking says: this page may be relevant. An AI recommendation says: this brand may be worth considering. That second one carries more weight, because people don’t want more information anymore, they want less confusion. They want a shortlist, not fifteen tabs.
The Data already Points this Way
Pew’s numbers above already make the point. Worth adding: longer, question-based searches trigger AI summaries far more often, 53% of the time for 10+ word searches, 60% for searches starting with who/what/when/why. That’s exactly how high-intent buyers search. This is where AI summaries get dangerous for brands that are searchable but not trusted.
A 2026 arXiv study on Google AI Overviews (55,393 queries, 19 categories) found activation jumped from 13.7% overall to 64.7% for question-form queries, and nearly 30% of cited domains never appeared on page one of Google. Being cited by AI isn’t the same as ranking on page one.
My Take: the old SEO map isn’t enough anymore. Ranking, technical SEO, and backlinks still matter, but now we also need to ask if our brand is machine-readable and trustworthy enough for AI to select in the first place. I break this down further in my SEO + AEO + GEO framework, where SEO drives discoverability, AEO drives answer extraction, and GEO drives AI citation.
Most Brands are Searchable but NOT RECOMMENDABLE!
A brand can have a website, post on Instagram, run Google Ads, rank for a few keywords, and publish blogs every week, and still not show up when someone asks ChatGPT, Gemini, or Perplexity for a recommendation.
Search engines can find your website. AI systems need to actually understand your brand, and most brands aren’t easy to understand. Their homepage says something like: we are a customer-centric, innovation-driven, end-to-end digital transformation partner helping businesses unlock growth.
That sounds polished. It says almost nothing. What do you actually do? Who do you help? What problem do you solve? What proof do you have? Why should someone trust you over five other options?
If a human can’t understand your brand in ten seconds, don’t expect AI to explain it properly.
The Next Phase of SEO is AI Visibility
I’ve always believed performance marketing should be judged by outcomes, not vanity metrics: leads, revenue, ROAS, CPA, qualified pipeline. Now I’d add one more layer, recommendation visibility. Does AI mention your brand for category questions? Does it place you correctly, compare you fairly, and trust your footprint enough to include you at all?
This isn’t about gaming AI. It’s about becoming easier to understand and easier to trust.
Gartner predicted traditional search volume would drop 25% by 2026 because of AI chatbots and virtual agents, and said companies would need to lean on content that demonstrates real expertise and trustworthiness. That lines up with what I’m seeing on the ground. The future isn’t more content, it’s more useful, credible, experience-led content.
For ecommerce, this is more urgent. OpenAI says more shopping journeys now start inside ChatGPT. Product data isn’t backend housekeeping anymore, it’s marketing infrastructure: clear titles, real reviews, comparison-friendly content. “Glow serum” isn’t enough. Say who it’s for and what it solves.
Ads can amplify a good offer. They can’t fix a confused brand. They bring traffic, not trust.
A Test for You
Your Google ranking is still valuable. It’s just not the full picture anymore. Next time your team celebrates a keyword ranking, ask one more question: when a customer asks ChatGPT for help in our category, are we part of the answer?
Open ChatGPT or Gemini right now and ask one real buying question from your category, not your brand name. See if you show up, how you’re described, and who shows up instead.
